steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The skill is Command of Evidence (Textual). The claim is that Aristophanes satirizes sophists as teaching students to be persuasive without regard for what is morally good. The correct quotation must show a sophist (or someone hiring one) explicitly prioritizing persuasion over moral goodness.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Socrates on suspending his intellect in a basket: This quotation describes an unusual method of study, not instruction in winning arguments regardless of morality. It does not support the satirical claim. ✗
  • Choice B — Strepsiades urging his son to go learn from a sophist: This shows a parent encouraging attendance, but the quotation contains no mention of immoral argument or disregard for what is good. ✗
  • Choice C — Socrates asking a new customer what he wants to learn: This is a generic intake question. It says nothing about teaching unethical persuasion, so it does not illustrate the satirical claim. ✗
  • Choice D — Strepsiades telling the sophist to teach his son the unjust style of argument: The quotation explicitly names “the worse” style that “by maintaining what is unjust, overturns the better,” and demands the sophist teach it “by all means.” This directly illustrates sophists teaching persuasion without regard for moral good. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
